title: The Te-Form: Requests & Progressive
learningGoal: The learner will master the most complex conjugation in beginner Japanese (the Te-form), categorize verbs into the three verb groups, make polite requests, and describe ongoing actions.
instructions: This is arguably the most important grammar lesson in all of Japanese. The 'Te-Form' allows you to link verbs together, ask people for favors, and say what you are currently doing. You MUST memorize the verb group rules.
conceptTitle: The Heart of Japanese Verbs: The Te-Form

lessonContent.bodyText: By itself, the Te-form means nothing. It is a connecting form. However, when you attach words like 'kudasai' (please) or 'imasu' (is doing) to it, it unlocks massive communicative power. To conjugate a verb into the Te-form, you must first know which 'Group' the verb belongs to.
Group 2 is easy! Just drop 'masu' and add 'te'. Group 3 only has two verbs: Shimasu (to do) and Kimasu (to come). Memorize them.

Group 1 conjugation depends on the syllable right before 'masu'. You must memorize this song/pattern:
1) i, chi, ri -> tte (Double T)
2) mi, bi, ni -> nde
3) ki -> ite
4) gi -> ide
5) shi -> shite

targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: Go and... (Ikite is wrong. It must be Itte)

lessonContent.bodyText: To ask someone politely to do something, conjugate the verb into the Te-form and add 'kudasai'.

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: これを たべて ください。
ちょっと まって ください。
えいごを はなして ください。

lessonContent.bodyText: To say you are CURRENTLY doing something (progressive tense like '-ing' in English), use the Te-form + 'imasu'.

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: いま ごはんを たべて います。
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: I am eating a meal now.

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: ほんを よんで います。
えいごを べんきょうして います。

learningObjective: The learner will correctly categorize verbs into their respective conjugation groups, execute the Te-form conjugations from memory, and form sentences requesting actions and describing current states.

examples.targetText: カフェで コーヒーを のんで います。ちょっと まって ください。
examples.transliteration: Kafe de koohii wo nonde imasu. Chotto matte kudasai.
examples.baseTranslation: I am drinking coffee at the cafe. Please wait a little.
examples.explanation: Combines location particle (de), object particle (wo), progressive tense (nonde imasu), and a request (matte kudasai).

importantRules.ruleText: Ikimasu (to go) is a Group 1 exception. Even though the syllable before masu is 'ki' (which normally becomes 'ite'), Ikimasu becomes 'Itte'.
importantRules.exceptionNotes: Saying 'Ikite kudasai' means 'Please live' (from ikimasu - to live). 'Itte kudasai' means 'Please go'.

importantRules.ruleText: Do not confuse Group 2 with Group 1 verbs that end in 'i'. E.g., 'Mimasu' is Group 2 (Mite). 'Kaimasu' is Group 1 (Katte).
importantRules.exceptionNotes: You will eventually memorize which is which by exposure.

cultureContext.contextText: 'Kudasai' is a polite request, but it is still a command (Please do this for me). You use it with equals, friends, or shop staff. Do not use 'Kudasai' to command your boss or a strict teacher to do something.
dosList: Use '~te kudasai' when asking someone for a favor or ordering food.
dontsList: Do not use '~te kudasai' to superiors unless it is clearly for their benefit (e.g. 'Please sit down').

summary: The Te-form connects verbs. Group 2 drops 'masu' and adds 'te'. Group 1 uses complex phonetic changes (i/chi/ri -> tte, mi/bi/ni -> nde, ki -> ite, gi -> ide, shi -> shite). Attach 'kudasai' for requests, and 'imasu' for ongoing actions.

checkpointQuestions.questionText: What is the Te-form of 'Nomimasu' (to drink)?

checkpointQuestions.expectedAnswer: まって ください (Matte kudasai)

checkpointQuestions.questionText: What is the Te-form of the exception verb 'Ikimasu' (to go)?

keyTakeaways: Group 1 conjugations: i/chi/ri(tte), mi/bi/ni(nde), ki(ite), gi(ide), shi(shite).
adminCorrectAnswerSet.conceptSummary: High-density block introducing verb conjugations and linking grammar.
adminCorrectAnswerSet.expectedUnderstanding: Learner must fluidly convert ~masu verbs into ~te verbs on the fly.

feedbackRules.onPass: Incredible job! You survived the hardest grammar wall in beginner Japanese.
feedbackRules.onFail: Do not rush this. Memorize the Group 1 song. i-chi-ri-tte, mi-bi-ni-nde, ki-ite, gi-ide, shi-shite.
feedbackRules.onPartial: Good start. Make sure you don't confuse Group 2 (drop masu add te) with Group 1.

audioAssets.audioTranscript: たべます、たべて。します、して。かいます、かって。まちます、まって。のみます、のんで。ききます、きいて。いきます、いって。これを たべて ください。ちょっと まって ください。ほんを よんで います。
